title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Bharat Steel & Alloys Ltd. and Anr.
court: High Court of Judicature at Allahabad
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no proper show cause notice was served and no opportunity of personal hearing was granted prior to dismissal.
The domestic enquiry conducted by the respondent company was vitiated as the Enquiry Officer was not impartial, being a direct subordinate of the complainant, thereby rendering the findings perverse.

The charges of misconduct levelled against the petitioner were not substantiated by any cogent or reliable evidence on record, and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate to the alleged misconduct.

The petitioner had completed over fourteen years of continuous service and was entitled to protection under Section 6-N of the U.P. Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, which was entirely bypassed by the employer.
The respondent employer failed to pay retrenchment compensation and notice pay as mandated under the relevant provisions of labour law, further rendering the termination illegal and void ab initio.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Allahabad held that the termination of the petitioner-employee, a Senior Technical Operator with fourteen years of service, was illegal and violative of principles of natural justice. The Court found that the domestic enquiry was conducted in a biased manner and the charges were unproven. The respondent company was directed to reinstate the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service from the date of termination.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT ALLAHABAD
Writ Petition No. 14872 of 2022

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
Versus
BHARAT STEEL AND ALLOYS LTD. AND ANR. ... Respondents

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARVIND PRASAD SINGH

JUDGMENT
Date of Decision: 17th March, 2023

1. The petitioner, Ramesh Kumar Sharma, has preferred this writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the order of dismissal dated 04.08.2021 passed by the Management of Bharat Steel and Alloys Ltd., Kanpur, and seeking reinstatement with full back wages and continuity of service.

2. Brief facts as emerging from the record are that the petitioner was employed as a Senior Technical Operator since 12.03.2007. On 18.06.2021, a charge sheet was issued alleging that the petitioner had been negligent in handling machinery, resulting in minor equipment damage. The petitioner submitted his reply denying all charges. A domestic enquiry was conducted by Shri D.K. Verma, who served as a direct reporting officer to the complainant supervisor. On the basis of the enquiry report, the petitioner was dismissed from service vide order dated 04.08.2021.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Sri Anand Tripathi, has vehemently argued that the entire enquiry proceedings were vitiated due to the bias of the Enquiry Officer and that no independent witness corroborated the charges. It is further argued that the respondent company circumvented the mandatory requirements of Section 6-N of the U.P. Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, and no retrenchment compensation was tendered.

4. Learned counsel for the respondent, Sri P.K. Mehrotra, has contended that the enquiry was conducted fairly and the findings are based on evidence. It is submitted that the petitioner was afforded sufficient opportunity to present his case.

5. This Court has carefully perused the entire material on record including the enquiry proceedings, the charge sheet, the petitioner's reply, and the impugned order. It is noted that the Enquiry Officer, Shri D.K. Verma, was a direct subordinate of the very supervisor whose complaint formed the basis of the charge sheet. The settled position in law, as enunciated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Union of India v. P.K. Roy, AIR 1968 SC 850 and subsequently affirmed in numerous decisions, is that an enquiry conducted by a biased or interested officer is fundamentally flawed and cannot be sustained.

6. Furthermore, this Court finds that no independent or reliable evidence was placed before the Enquiry Officer to substantiate the charge of negligence. The sole testimony relied upon was that of the complainant supervisor, which is inherently interested. The principle that punishment must be commensurate with proved misconduct is well established. In the present case, even assuming the charge were proved, dismissal of a fourteen-year employee for a minor operational lapse would be grossly disproportionate.

7. On the question of compliance with Section 6-N of the U.P. Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, this Court finds that the respondent admittedly did not follow the statutory procedure for retrenchment, nor was any compensation offered or paid. The termination is therefore rendered void ab initio on this ground independently.

8. In view of the foregoing discussion, this Court is of the considered opinion that the impugned order of dismissal dated 04.08.2021 cannot be sustained in law. The writ petition is accordingly allowed. The respondent company is directed to reinstate the petitioner to his original post with all consequential benefits including full back wages and continuity of service with effect from the date of dismissal.

9. The respondents shall comply with this order within a period of eight weeks from the date of receipt of a certified copy of this judgment.

10. No order as to costs.

(ARVIND PRASAD SINGH, J.)
Date: 17.03.2023
